g.data-narration: Welcome to Transaction Processing - the backbone of reliable database management systems ensuring data integrity.
g.text: ACID Properties • Transaction States • Concurrency Control

g.data-narration: A transaction is a logical unit of work containing database operations that transforms data from one consistent state to another.
g.text: A logical unit of work consisting of one or more database operations
Transforms database from one consistent state to another

g.data-narration: Transaction processing ensures data integrity in banking, reservations, and e-commerce systems where reliability is critical.
g.text: Without transactions: Data corruption, inconsistent states

g.data-narration: ACID properties define valid transactions: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability ensure reliable database operations.
g.text: ACID Properties - The Foundation of Transactions

g.data-narration: Atomicity ensures all operations execute as a single unit - either all succeed or none do, with rollback on failure.
g.text: Transaction: Transfer Rs.1000 from Account A to B
If system fails after Step 2 → Rollback: A=5000 restored

g.data-narration: Consistency ensures transactions maintain all database constraints, transforming valid states to valid states only.
g.text: Consistency - Valid State to Valid State
Constraint: Total Balance = Savings + Checking must always equal 8000
✗ If violation occurs → Transaction aborted, database restored

g.data-narration: Isolation ensures concurrent transactions appear serial - intermediate results remain invisible to other transactions.
Durability guarantees committed transaction effects persist permanently, surviving system crashes through logging.
Transactions move through five states: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, and Aborted during their lifecycle.
Concurrency problems include Lost Update where one transaction overwrites another's changes, and Dirty Read of uncommitted data.
g.text: Concurrency Problems: Lost Update & Dirty Read

g.data-narration: Non-Repeatable Read occurs when a transaction sees different values on repeated reads of the same data.
g.text: More Concurrency: Non-Repeatable Read & Phantom Read
T1: SELECT * FROM Accts WHERE type='SAVINGS' → 100 rows
T2: INSERT INTO Accts VALUES(...) → New row inserted
T1: SELECT * FROM Accts WHERE type='SAVINGS' → 101 rows!

g.data-narration: Schedules define operation order in concurrent transactions. Serial schedules execute transactions sequentially, ensuring consistency.
Lock-based protocols use Shared and Exclusive locks. Two-Phase Locking ensures serializability through growing and shrinking phases.
Key takeaways: ACID properties ensure reliability, transactions have five states, and concurrency requires careful control mechanisms.
g.text: • ACID: Atomicity (all-or-nothing) • Consistency (valid states) • Isolation (serial appearance) • Durability (permanent)
• States: Active → Partially Committed → Committed (or Failed → Aborted)
• 2PL: Growing Phase (acquire) → Shrinking Phase (release) ensures conflict-serializability

g.data-narration: Remember: ACID is essential, serial schedules are always correct, locks prevent concurrency problems, and 2PL ensures serializability.
g.text: 🎯 Memorize ACID • Know transaction state order • Serial = always correct • S-lock allows concurrent read, X-lock blocks all
